title: Sheet Pan Shrimp Boil (High-Protein Recipe) | Eati
introduction: Sheet Pan Shrimp Boil is a fast, high-protein dinner that brings classic boil flavor with less cleanup. Shrimp, potatoes, and seasoning come together on a single sheet pan you can log in Eati.

sections.heading: Ingredients
sections.content:
- Shrimp
- Potatoes
- Sausage optional
- Seasoning blend
- Garlic
- Lemon
- Vegetables (as desired)
- Salt and pepper

sections.heading: Instructions
sections.content:
1. Preheat oven and prep a sheet pan.
2. Toss potatoes with oil, garlic, and seasoning; roast until partially tender.
3. Add shrimp (and sausage/vegetables if using) and toss again.
4. Roast until shrimp is cooked and vegetables are tender.
5. Finish with lemon, serve, and log your portion in Eati.

sections.heading: Calories, Fat, and Protein (Estimated)
sections.content:
Calories: 296 kcal per serving (estimated)
Fat: 12.5g
Protein: 27g

Tip: keep sausage optional and prioritize shrimp for a leaner profile.

sections.heading: Why This Lightens a Classic Southern Comfort Food
sections.content:
A traditional Louisiana shrimp boil is an hours-long event — 5-gallon pot, corn, potatoes, sausage, and often 2-3 lbs of shrimp per person. It's amazing. It's also a 1,000+ calorie meal with heavy sausage and butter-slathered potatoes.

Traditional shrimp boil (1 plate):
• 900–1,200 calories
• 35g protein
• 50g fat (often from andouille + butter)
• High sodium (2,000+ mg)

This sheet pan version:
• 296 calories
• 27g protein
• 12.5g fat
• Moderate sodium (500–700 mg)

You save 600+ calories without losing the classic flavor profile.

How the calorie reduction works:
• Less sausage: 1 oz per serving instead of 4 oz (saves 200+ cal).
• Controlled oil: 1 tbsp total per serving instead of butter sticks (saves 150+ cal).
• No post-cook butter drizzle: Saves 100+ cal.
• Regular potato portion: 6 oz per serving instead of 'eat till you burst' (saves 100+ cal).

Why sheet pan beats boiling:
• Less water-logging of shrimp (maintains texture).
• Flavor concentrates instead of leaching into water.
• Easy portion control.
• Way less cleanup.
• Still gets that classic Old Bay seasoning hit.

Protein-per-calorie: 9g per 100 cal — solid, and the shrimp portion can be scaled up if you need more protein. Use a protein calculator to check your targets.

sections.heading: The Andouille Sausage Decision
sections.content:
Traditional shrimp boils use andouille sausage — a spicy Cajun pork sausage. But standard andouille is calorie-heavy (about 300 cal per 4 oz).

Sausage options ranked for macro-friendliness:

Chicken andouille (Aidells, Al Fresco):
• 140 cal, 14g protein per 3 oz.
• Closest to traditional taste with lower calories.
• Best overall choice.

Turkey andouille (Jennie-O, Butterball):
• 120 cal, 16g protein per 3 oz.
• Leanest option, still spicy.
• Great for strict fat-loss phases.

Kielbasa (turkey):
• 140 cal, 14g protein per 3 oz.
• Milder flavor but works well.

Traditional pork andouille:
• 250 cal, 13g protein per 3 oz.
• Classic flavor; higher calories.
• Best for splurge meals.

Pork kielbasa:
• 300 cal, 12g protein per 3 oz.
• Milder than andouille.
• Not recommended for fat loss.

Vegetarian sausage:
• 140 cal, 16g protein per 3 oz (Beyond/Impossible).
• Works surprisingly well with seafood boil seasonings.

No sausage option:
• Save 100+ cal per serving.
• Add extra shrimp to maintain protein.
• Consider adding extra smoked paprika to compensate for smoky flavor.

For this recipe:
• Include 1 oz sausage for authentic flavor.
• Use chicken or turkey andouille.
• Slice thinly to distribute flavor through entire dish.

If you want to keep sausage prominent:
Scale up to 2 oz per serving but reduce potatoes to compensate — sausage has more flavor per calorie than plain potatoes.

sections.heading: Perfect Potato Technique for Sheet Pan Cooking
sections.content:
Potatoes are the trickiest component — they take much longer to cook than shrimp. Timing is everything.

Potato varieties ranked for this recipe:

Baby red potatoes (best):
• Firm, waxy texture holds up to roasting.
• 'Cuts' easily (usually just halved).
• Traditional choice for boils.
• 90 cal per 3 oz.

Baby Yukon Gold:
• Buttery flavor, creamy interior.
• Similar behavior to baby red.
• 95 cal per 3 oz.

Fingerlings:
• Elegant presentation.
• Slightly longer cooking.
• Can be slightly higher calorie by weight.

Russet potatoes:
• Not recommended — texture gets mealy when baked this way.
• Better for mashing or french fries.

Sweet potato variation:
• 100 cal per 3 oz.
• Tender in 20 min (faster than regular potatoes).
• Sweet contrast to spicy shrimp.
• Great for a healthier twist.

Potato cooking timing:

Step 1: Pre-cook potatoes
• Option A: Parboil 10 minutes.
• Option B: Microwave 5 minutes.
• Option C: Pre-roast 20 minutes at 425°F before adding shrimp.

This is essential. Shrimp cooks in 8 minutes. Potatoes take 25+ minutes alone. Without pre-cooking, you'll either have raw potatoes or rubbery shrimp.

Step 2: Combine with seasoning
• Toss par-cooked potatoes in oil + Old Bay + garlic + paprika.
• Spread on sheet pan, broil 2 min for color.

Step 3: Add shrimp and sausage
• Sausage: Add with potatoes — it's pre-cooked, just heats and crisps.
• Shrimp: Add in last 6–8 minutes.

Step 4: Finish
• Squeeze lemon, garnish with parsley.
• Serve immediately.

Cutting technique:
• Potatoes under 1 inch: Halve.
• 1–2 inch potatoes: Quarter.
• 2+ inch potatoes: Choose smaller ones or cut into 1-inch chunks.
• Even sizing = even cooking.

sections.heading: Old Bay and Seasoning Alternatives
sections.content:
Old Bay is the classic shrimp boil seasoning, but there are great alternatives if you don't have it.

Old Bay ingredients (and what it tastes like):
• Celery salt
• Paprika
• Mustard
• Red pepper
• Black pepper
• Bay leaf
• Cloves, allspice, ginger, mace, cardamom

Seasoning options:

1. Old Bay (classic, reach for first):
2 tbsp per recipe. Most authentic.

2. Zatarain's Crab Boil:
More aggressive heat. Concentrated — use 1 tbsp.

3. Tony Chachere's:
Cajun-style. Saltier and peppery.

4. Homemade 'Old Bay dupe':
• 1 tbsp smoked paprika
• 2 tsp celery salt
• 2 tsp mustard powder
• 1 tsp black pepper
• 1/2 tsp cayenne
• 1/2 tsp allspice
• 1/4 tsp ground cloves

Mix and use 2 tbsp per recipe.

5. Creole seasoning:
Similar flavor profile, slightly more heat.

Spice level variations:

Mild:
• 1.5 tbsp Old Bay
• 1 tsp black pepper
• No cayenne

Medium (default):
• 2 tbsp Old Bay
• 1 tsp black pepper
• 1/4 tsp cayenne

Hot:
• 2 tbsp Old Bay
• 1/2 tsp cayenne
• 1 tsp red pepper flakes
• 1 tsp hot sauce

Nuclear (Cajun-style):
• 2 tbsp Old Bay
• 1 tbsp cayenne
• 2 tsp red pepper flakes
• 1 tsp ghost pepper (optional)

Regional variations:

Louisiana-style:
• More cayenne and garlic
• Paprika-heavy
• Typically with andouille

Maryland/Chesapeake:
• Old Bay dominant
• Less heat, more celery salt and mustard

Low Country (South Carolina/Georgia):
• Simpler: bay leaves, lemon, black pepper
• Focus on seafood quality

South Louisiana 'crawfish boil' style:
• Add whole garlic bulbs
• Lemon wedges
• Ears of corn
• Heavy on Zatarain's

sections.heading: Serving Ideas, Batching, and Storage
sections.content:
Shrimp boil is best fresh but can work for meal prep with the right approach.

Traditional serving (for groups):
• Spread newspaper or parchment on table.
• Pour shrimp boil directly onto table.
• Serve with lemon wedges, cocktail sauce, hot sauce, crusty bread.
• Guests grab what they want.
• Super fun for gatherings.

Portion control tip: 4 oz shrimp + 6 oz potato + 1 oz sausage = 1 serving.

Low-calorie pairings:
• Mixed green salad (80 cal)
• Grilled corn (80 cal per cob)
• Cucumber salad (40 cal per cup)
• Coleslaw, light version (120 cal per cup)

Bread pairing (for authentic feel):
• Whole-grain roll (120 cal)
• Small piece of garlic bread (150 cal)
• Corn bread muffin (170 cal)

Complete plate examples:

Fat loss (400 cal, 30g protein):
• Sheet pan shrimp boil (296)
• Mixed green salad with vinaigrette (80)
• Lemon wedge, hot sauce (free)

Maintenance (550 cal, 33g protein):
• Sheet pan shrimp boil (296)
• 1 ear grilled corn (80)
• Whole grain roll (120)
• Cocktail sauce (50)

Feast (750 cal, 40g protein):
• Shrimp boil with extra shrimp (400)
• 1 ear corn (80)
• Corn bread (170)
• 1 tbsp melted butter drizzle (100)

Dipping sauces (use sparingly):
• Cocktail sauce (20 cal per tbsp)
• Spicy mustard (5 cal per tbsp)
• Greek yogurt remoulade (light version, 30 cal per tbsp)
• Hot sauce (nearly 0 cal)
• Melted butter (100 cal per tbsp — use wisely)

Meal prep approach:
• Shrimp is best fresh — don't make ahead for week.
• Potatoes and sausage reheat well.
• Strategy: Prep potatoes Sunday, reheat on pan with fresh shrimp.

Storage:
• Fridge: 2 days max (shrimp degrades quickly).
• Freezer: Not recommended — destroys texture.

Reheating:
• Oven: 350°F for 8 min.
• Skillet: 4 min medium heat.
• Microwave: 90 seconds but shrimp may overcook.

Leftover ideas:
• Chop into shrimp salad with Greek yogurt.
• Add to pasta.
• Top a grain bowl.
• Stuff into tacos with coleslaw.

For another shrimp recipe, see [baked shrimp and broccoli foil packs](/blog/baked-shrimp-and-broccoli-foil-packs-recipe) and [easy miso salmon](/blog/easy-miso-salmon-recipe).

conclusion: Less mess, strong protein, and bold flavor. Use Eati to portion the meal so it matches your calorie and protein targets.
metaTitle: Sheet Pan Shrimp Boil: 27g Protein Cajun-Style Dinner (600 Cal Saved)
metaDescription: Lighter sheet pan shrimp boil with 27g protein in 296 calories — saves 600+ cal vs traditional. Includes sausage comparison, perfect potato technique, and Old Bay alternatives.
targetKeyword: sheet pan shrimp boil high protein

faqs.question: How many calories in sheet pan shrimp boil?
faqs.answer: Approximately 296 calories per serving with 27g of protein and 12.5g of fat. This is 60–70% fewer calories than a traditional Louisiana shrimp boil (900–1,200 cal per plate), while maintaining the classic flavor profile and protein content.

faqs.question: What's the best sausage for shrimp boil?
faqs.answer: For fat-loss goals, chicken andouille (140 cal per 3 oz) or turkey andouille (120 cal per 3 oz). For authentic flavor with moderate calories, traditional pork andouille (250 cal per 3 oz) at smaller portion. Slice thin so flavor distributes through the entire pan.

faqs.question: Can I make shrimp boil without Old Bay?
faqs.answer: Yes. Substitute with Zatarain's, Creole seasoning, Tony Chachere's, or make your own blend: smoked paprika, celery salt, mustard powder, black pepper, cayenne, allspice, and a pinch of ground cloves. Each gives slightly different character.

faqs.question: How do I keep shrimp from overcooking?
faqs.answer: Add shrimp only in the last 6–8 minutes. Watch for color change to opaque pink with slight C-shape curl (tight O-shape = overcooked). Internal temp of 120°F = done. Pre-cook potatoes before adding shrimp so timing works out.

faqs.question: Is shrimp boil keto?
faqs.answer: Not as written — potatoes make it high-carb. For keto: substitute cauliflower florets and turnip chunks for potatoes. Keep shrimp, sausage, and seasoning same. Result: about 280 calories, 27g protein, 8g net carbs per serving.
